transcript.text: I’m a data analyst with around three years of experience at a mid‑size firm. I took the qualifier test because I was curious whether the IIM Ranchi program could offer any fresh insights or help me progress further in my career.
introduce yourself

I’m a data analyst with about three years of experience in analytics at a mid‑size company. I cleared the qualifier test because I wanted to see if an IIM Ranchi certification could add real value to my current skill set and career growth.
What are you looking for?

I’m looking to deepen my expertise in applying machine‑learning and AI at a strategic level – essentially moving from execution to leadership on analytics projects. I’d like to know how the ML/AI modules are structured for someone already working in the field, and how employers typically view an IIM Ranchi certificate for an experienced analyst.
Thank you.

That makes sense. Could you tell me exactly what topics the “Machine Learning for Business — Foundations” and “Advanced Analytics, AI Applications and Leadership” modules cover? Also, is the program geared more toward beginners, or is there flexibility for professionals like me who already have a solid analytics background?

Yes, this program is designed for all we start from foundations so that even if you are not having that round in that course, you will be able to understand it from the foundations only and then we will go deep into that when you are going through the course.

Got it. Could you break down the specific topics covered in the “Machine Learning for Business — Foundations” module – for example, which algorithms, tools, or case studies are included? And similarly, what does the “Advanced Analytics, AI Applications and Leadership” module focus on – are there leadership frameworks, particular industry use‑cases, or hands‑on projects? Also, do employers generally view this certification as a differentiator for someone already working in analytics?

phaseBreakdown.name: Opening
phaseBreakdown.summary: Opening: Counsellor greeted Prashant by name, acknowledged BCom second year, and pivoted to an open question about how interest in analytics started.
phaseBreakdown.didWell: Used the student's name correctly and confirmed background accurately, avoiding the disjointed level-1 anchor.
phaseBreakdown.toImprove: Missed the chance to hand the wheel to the student ('any queries from your end?') and build a personal beat around Pune, BCom, or his story before pivoting to business.

phaseBreakdown.name: Discovery
phaseBreakdown.summary: Discovery: A single open-ended question was asked about how interest developed; the student revealed he had no real interest and no plan.
phaseBreakdown.didWell: Asked a non-leading, curiosity-driven question that elicited an honest answer.
phaseBreakdown.toImprove: Should have followed up with goal, family context, expected package, timeline, and what 'curiosity' means to him — the call effectively died at this point.

phaseBreakdown.name: Presentation
phaseBreakdown.summary: Presentation: No programme content was presented at all.
phaseBreakdown.didWell: Nothing positive — no presentation occurred.

phaseBreakdown.didWell: No coercive or dismissive language was used — the counsellor did not steamroll.
phaseBreakdown.toImprove: Should have validated the honesty, reframed 'curiosity' as a legitimate entry point, and pre-empted parent/fee/trust concerns before they could harden.

phaseBreakdown.name: Close

phaseBreakdown.didWell: Did not apply pressure or invent scarcity — credit for not coercing.
phaseBreakdown.toImprove: Should have at least proposed a low-friction next step (a campus-immersive webinar, a callback with parents, a 24-hour think-it-over) instead of letting the call end cold.

drills.title: Mirror-before-question drill
drills.instruction: For 5 mock openings with a working professional persona, your first two sentences must restate the student's stated context (role, goal, trigger) before asking any question. Practice until mirror-then-ask becomes automatic; then layer on the four core discovery questions (current role, why now, family awareness, target outcome).

drills.title: Programme-walk drill on a 90-second clock

drills.title: Seat-block ask with 10-day balance window

Run it 8 times until the ask lands at roughly 75–80% of the call timeline without sounding coercive.

overall.outcomeDetail: The student did not pay the ₹3,000 seat-block; after the abusive language in turns 15–21, the wrong fee amount in turn 25, and a complete failure to address the AI/analytics fit concern, Vikram explicitly declined and ended the call.

rubric.label: Rapport & Opening
rubric.justification: Matches the 1-anchor: trust was destroyed within the first few minutes — the counsellor opened with the wrong self-introduction ('Hutaifat said'), then escalated to abusive language in turn 15 ('भाई तेरे लिए कोर्स नहीं बना तू चला जा') and turn 19 ('अबे गांड मारा भाग'), making the opening disjointed and hostile rather than a genuine connection.

rubric.label: Needs Discovery
rubric.justification: The counsellor asked exactly one qualifying question ('why you wanted to enroll into this course?') in turn 1 and never probed the analytics-vs-entrepreneurship tension the student repeatedly raised, then told him the course is 'not for you' in turn 17 — closer to the 1-anchor of pitching around the student's stated need than to deep discovery.

rubric.label: Programme Presentation
rubric.justification: Turn 5 listed the seven modules in Hindi in roughly the right order, but Module 03 ('Leveraging AI for Smarter Startups') was described only as a generic tool demo in turn 11 ('all the latest tools that are in the market…handle your data more efficiently'), missing the AI-leverage framing and never tailoring the pitch to Vikram's analytics background — a one-size-fits-all delivery closer to a 2 than a 3.

rubric.label: Objection Handling
rubric.justification: Matches the 1-anchor: when the student said the AI/entrepreneurship fit was wrong, the counsellor steamrolled by telling him to leave ('I think this course is not for you. You can, uh, probably leave this, uh, counseling call') instead of engaging the concern, and when the student asked to continue, the counsellor immediately jumped to a payment ask with no acknowledgment of the fit concern.

rubric.label: Product Knowledge & Accuracy
rubric.justification: The module list in turn 5 was broadly accurate, but the seat-block amount was quoted as 'around 4,000 rupees' in turn 25 when the actual non-refundable seat-block is ₹3,000 — a direct factual contradiction of the ground-truth fee structure, and the AI module was mischaracterised as a tools-only prototyping block.

rubric.label: Closing & Payment Ask
rubric.justification: Matches the 1-anchor: the closing was essentially coercive and confusing — coming right after an apology for abusive language, the counsellor asked for the wrong amount (₹4,000 instead of the actual ₹3,000 seat-block) with no context, no deadline framing, and no walk-through of the payment steps.

rubric.label: Communication & Empathy
rubric.justification: Matches the 1-anchor pattern of talking down — the counsellor used abusive and mocking language ('भाई तेरे लिए कोर्स नहीं बना तू चला जा', 'अबे गांड मारा भाग', 'मेरे को रिस्पॉन्ड कर तू कुछ बावड़ी तरेड'), then laughed it off and pretended a colleague said it, showing zero empathy for the working professional's stated upskilling need.

rubric.label: Voice Delivery
rubric.justification: Matches the 1-anchor: pace swung wildly from 98 wpm to 256 wpm across turns, sentences were fragmented ('it does- uh, it does actually bind a lot of AI things into- uh, uh…'), and the counsellor talked over the student's confusion in turns 15–21 instead of pausing — leaving the transcript hard to follow and the student visibly frustrated.

drills.title: Recovery from an emotional rupture mid-call
drills.instruction: Pair with a coach who will deliberately insult or mock you on a practice call. Your job is to (1) pause, (2) name what just happened ('I think that came across as disrespectful and I'm sorry'), (3) ask the student how they'd like to proceed, and (4) only resume selling after explicit re-opt-in. Practice until the recovery feels natural under 15 seconds.

drills.title: Memorise and rehearse the exact fee script

drills.title: Reframe a fit-mismatch objection instead of dismissing it
drills.instruction: Record 5 mock calls where the student is a working professional in analytics/data who says 'this is not for me, I need AI upskilling'. Your job is to never say 'this course is not for you'. Instead, re-anchor: surface how their domain lens is the actual unfair advantage in Module 03 (AI for startups) and Module 04 (prototyping). Review the recordings and tag every dismissal phrase you catch.

rubric.justification: Anchors between 1 and 3 — Counsellor only asks surface-level qualification (course name, how the test went, what info they have) and never probes the student's real situation despite the explicit signal in turn 3 ("matlab main bas soch raha tha ki abhi kya karna hai") that he is directionless after graduation.

rubric.label: Objection Handling

rubric.justification: Anchors 1 — Counsellor brands the programme as from "IIMs" (it is XLRI) and quotes duration as "around three months" (real duration is 6 months) in turn 10, both being explicit contradictions of ground-truth course facts.

phaseBreakdown.name: Opening
phaseBreakdown.summary: Opening — Counsellor greeted Vikram by name and asked how he was, then immediately moved to ask which course he was interested in. No personal connection was made; typo in greeting ("Hei") and tense opening tone weakened the first impression.
phaseBreakdown.didWell: Confirmed the student's name and stated the session's purpose early.
phaseBreakdown.toImprove: Hand the wheel to the student, ask about his current situation before pitching, and fix the typo / opening tone to set a warmer frame.

phaseBreakdown.name: Discovery
phaseBreakdown.summary: Discovery — Counsellor asked which course, how the qualifier test went, and what info the student already had. Never explored why the student wants entrepreneurship, his current job search, or family/financial context.
phaseBreakdown.didWell: Confirmed the course the student is interested in and acknowledged he had qualified the test.
phaseBreakdown.toImprove: Probe the "abhi kya karna hai" sentiment — current activity, financial runway, family situation, target outcomes — before any pitch.

phaseBreakdown.name: Presentation
phaseBreakdown.summary: Presentation — Counsellor delivered a one-sentence overview that wrongly attributed the programme to IIMs and quoted a 3-month duration (real: XLRI, 6 months). No modules, projects, capstone, or outcomes were shared despite the student saying he had zero information.
phaseBreakdown.didWell: Did not jump straight to fees; attempted a top-line framing before going deeper.
phaseBreakdown.toImprove: Walk through the 7 modules briefly, name the institute correctly as XLRI, state 6 months, and tie each module to the student's stated need.

phaseBreakdown.name: Objections & Negotiation
phaseBreakdown.summary: Objections & Negotiation — No objections were raised or pre-empted. Counsellor answered the flexibility question (weekend classes, recordings) but the transcript ends with the student's campus-immersion question unanswered.
phaseBreakdown.didWell: Gave a clean, accurate answer on weekend scheduling and recording access in turn 12.
phaseBreakdown.toImprove: Pre-empt common concerns (fees, EMI, placement/career support, time commitment) and finish answering the campus-immersion question before moving on.

phaseBreakdown.name: Close
phaseBreakdown.summary: Close — No payment ask, no urgency, no next step. The conversation simply stopped with the student mid-question, far short of the 78% benchmark for the ask.
phaseBreakdown.didWell: Did not apply coercive pressure or false scarcity.
phaseBreakdown.toImprove: Anchor a specific follow-up time, explain the ₹3,000 seat-block vs ₹63,000 fee structure, and place the payment ask around the 78% mark of the call.

drills.title: One-Sentence Pitch Accuracy Drill
drills.instruction: Stand in front of a mirror or record yourself and deliver a 30-second opener that includes, in this exact order: institute (XLRI x Masai), duration (6 months), format (online + campus immersion), and the 7 module names. Repeat 10 times until it is word-perfect; any slip triggers a restart.

drills.title: Late-Call Payment Ask Roleplay
drills.instruction: Pair with a coach and run a 10-minute mock where the coach will deliberately keep steering back to content questions. Your job is to (a) keep answering briefly, (b) watch the clock, and (c) place the ₹3,000 seat-block ask between minutes 7 and 8 of a 10-minute call, anchored to a specific next step.

drills.title: Discovery-on-Confusion Drill
drills.instruction: When a student says "I don't know what to do," practice five follow-up probes in order: (1) What did you study and when did you finish? (2) Have you been interviewing or applying? (3) What does your family think you should be doing? (4) Financially, how long can you wait before earning? (5) What would "success" look like 12 months from now?

rubric.justification: Covered the standard checklist — work role (associate business analyst at Infosys, 2 years), tools (SQL, Power BI), and goal (GenAI for automation) — but never probed the 'why' behind her GenAI interest, expected package jump, or decision context, matching the 3-anchor of accurate but surface-level qualification.

rubric.label: Programme Presentation
rubric.justification: Delivered only fragments — 'six months program', 'weekend live sessions', '9-10 hours per week' — with no module-by-module walkthrough, no project mention, and no tie-back to her analytics work, falling between anchors 1 and 3: present but too thin to count as a structured overview.

rubric.label: Objection Handling
rubric.justification: Reacted to the two raised objections (EMI hidden charges in turn 25 and certificate authenticity in turn 29) with factual answers, matching the 3-anchor of reacting well but never anticipating concerns like time commitment, family buy-in, or career relevance.

rubric.label: Product Knowledge & Accuracy
rubric.justification: Stated the ₹4,000 seat-block fee is 'fully refundable in case you change your mind' — directly contradicting the ground truth that it is non-refundable — and labelled the EMI 'interest-free' without verification, both clear factual errors matching the 1-anchor of stating things that are simply false.

rubric.label: Closing & Payment Ask
rubric.justification: Made a timely payment ask around turn 28 (~72% in) and successfully sent a link the student used, matching the 3-anchor of a plain, effective ask that closes but leaves the next step (balance payment timeline, batch start logistics) entirely open-ended.

rubric.label: Communication & Empathy
rubric.justification: Polite, clear, and patient throughout, and handled the cybersecurity/GenAI pivot without judgment, but offered no real validation of Paulomi's situation beyond procedural courtesy, matching the 3-anchor of emotionally flat professionalism.

rubric.label: Voice Delivery
rubric.justification: Warm tone and intelligible pace (mostly 170-220 wpm) with clear enunciation, but fragmented phrasing like 'you are- you have obtained in cybersecurity and ethical hacking with applied AI' and the techno-music stings interrupt flow, matching the 3-anchor of clear and steady but functional rather than inviting.

phaseBreakdown.name: Opening
phaseBreakdown.summary: Opening with Hindi-mixed greeting and immediate name error 'Prashant' for student Paulomi.
phaseBreakdown.didWell: Confirmed audio connectivity and warm tone.
phaseBreakdown.toImprove: Verify the student's name from CRM/qualifier data before the call starts; never open with a wrong first name as it sets a trust deficit for the entire session.

phaseBreakdown.name: Discovery
phaseBreakdown.summary: Light discovery covering role, tenure, and tools, then pivoting off the cybersecurity track when the student flagged interest in GenAI.
phaseBreakdown.didWell: Caught the misalignment in turn 8 and offered the GenAI alternative in turn 10 without forcing the wrong product.
phaseBreakdown.toImprove: Go deeper — ask what manual reporting tasks she wants automated, what her 12-month outcome looks like, and whether her employer supports upskilling, to ground value in her world.

phaseBreakdown.name: Presentation
phaseBreakdown.summary: Programme overview covering IIT Roorkee association, 6-month duration, weekend live + recorded format, and 9-10 hours/week load.
phaseBreakdown.didWell: Addressed her 'beginner vs advanced' concern by positioning the course as foundation-to-advanced.
phaseBreakdown.toImprove: Walk through the actual curriculum modules, capstone, and campus immersion explicitly; tie a module to her Power BI/SQL background to make it concrete.

phaseBreakdown.name: Objections & Negotiation
phaseBreakdown.summary: Two objections handled: EMI transparency (turn 25) and certificate issuer/format (turn 29).
phaseBreakdown.didWell: Confirmed zero hidden charges and the physical IIT Roorkee certificate with optional campus visit — a genuine value-add she responded to warmly.
phaseBreakdown.toImprove: Pre-empt the obvious objections (time vs. weekend workload, family/manager buy-in, career switch credibility) instead of waiting for them.

phaseBreakdown.name: Close
phaseBreakdown.summary: Payment link sent in turn 34, ₹4,000 received, batch start confirmed as '20 days from now'.
phaseBreakdown.didWell: Secured the conversion cleanly with a single ask and a fast link drop.
phaseBreakdown.toImprove: Anchor the next step — give a specific balance-payment deadline, a calendar invite for orientation, and confirm the start date in writing rather than the vague '20 days from now'.

strengths.point: Responsive product pivot when the student's interest did not match the offered programme
strengths.quote: We also have our Gen AI programs. If you are interested in them, I can give you a brief around that.

strengths.point: Clear, warm tone that put a confused student at ease after the cybersecurity/GenAI mismatch
strengths.quote: So all of our program are designed in such a way that we start from very foundation al- foundation only

strengths.point: Closed the seat-block payment in a single, low-friction ask
strengths.quote: Surely I can send you a payment link on which you can do the payment. Once you are done with the payment, please confirm with me.

improvements.point: Never call the ₹4,000 seat-block fee refundable — it is non-refundable per policy and this is a material misrepresentation
improvements.quote: this amount will be fully refundable in case you change your mind going forward
improvements.suggestion: Memorise the exact refund policy: 'The ₹4,000 seat-block and ₹56,000 programme fee are both non-refundable; the ₹4,000 is adjustable against the ₹56,000 balance.' If unsure, say 'let me confirm with my team' rather than improvise.

improvements.point: Verify the student's name on the CRM tab before dialling — two name errors in one call (Prashant at open, Palme at close) signal carelessness
improvements.quote: हाय प्रशांत
improvements.suggestion: Open every call by reading the CRM header silently, then greet with the exact name on file. If pronunciation is uncertain, ask 'Am I saying your name right?' in the first 10 seconds.

improvements.point: Do not label the EMI 'interest-free' without checking the actual EMI total against the upfront fee
improvements.quote: you can give this on EMI as well, which is interest-free
improvements.suggestion: Quote the exact EMI number (9 × ₹7,156 = ₹64,404 plus 18% GST) and say 'no third-party interest' rather than 'interest-free' if any processing component is bundled in.

keyMoments.note: Wrong name 'Prashant' on a student named Paulomi — instant trust break in the first 10 seconds.
Clean, non-defensive pivot off the cybersecurity pitch onto GenAI after the student flagged confusion — saved the call from derailing.
Stated the ₹4,000 seat-block is 'fully refundable' when policy says it is non-refundable — a material factual error that could trigger a refund dispute later.
Second name error in the same call — 'Palme' instead of 'Paulomi' — reinforcing the impression of inattention even after conversion.

drills.title: Refund-Policy Role Play
drills.instruction: Pair with a coach who plays Paulomi and asks 'If I change my mind after paying 4,000, do I get it back?' Drill the exact policy line: 'The ₹4,000 seat-block is non-refundable but fully adjustable against the ₹56,000 balance, so you don't lose the money — you just apply it to the programme fee.' Record and review for the word 'non-refundable' being used clearly.

drills.title: First-Ten-Seconds Name Drill
drills.instruction: Run 10 cold-call role plays where the student's name is deliberately tricky (e.g. Paulomi, Sreeram, Xinyi). Before dialling, read the CRM header aloud; greet within 3 seconds using the exact spelling. Self-score each call on whether the name was used correctly, and replay any miss.

drills.title: EMI Transparency Drill
drills.instruction: Practise quoting both payment paths in one breath: '₹60,000 upfront or ₹7,156 × 9 = ₹64,404 via EMI, plus 18% GST on either option.' Coach plays a price-sensitive student who asks 'Is there really no interest?' Train the response: 'No third-party loan interest, but the EMI total is ₹4,404 higher than upfront to cover the instalment processing.'

improvements.suggestion: Follow the goal with: 'Helpful — what does a senior/product role look like for you in package and scope, and is there a target number or title you're aiming for in the next 12-18 months?'

improvements.point: Build value with a short, role-tailored programme overview and anchor a specific next step instead of letting the call drift to an open end.
improvements.quote: I just have a few minutes right now.
improvements.suggestion: Acknowledge the constraint, deliver a 60-second curated pitch (6 months, 8 modules, IIM Ranchi + Masai, online + campus immersion, one analytics-manager case), and close with a booked time: 'Can I block 20 minutes tomorrow at 6 PM to walk you through modules 4-7 that map to the product track?'

keyMoments.note: Disjointed opening ('How do I speak? Hello?') and abrupt command 'Now, can you introduce yourself?' erode first-impression trust with a senior working professional.
Student flags 'I just have a few minutes right now' and the counsellor neither acknowledges it nor tightens the agenda — a missed empathy and time-management beat.
Only a single generic 'What are you looking for from this course?' is asked; no follow-up to surface the specific technical gap, target package, or decision drivers.
Call ends without any presentation, payment ask, or anchored follow-up — converting a warm working-professional lead into a dead end.

drills.title: 60-Second Curated Pitch for a Time-Pressed Senior Analyst
drills.instruction: Role-play with a timer: open by acknowledging the student's stated time limit, then deliver exactly 60 seconds covering duration (6 months), format (online + campus immersion), the 8-module arc with two modules tied to a senior/product analytics role, and the IIM Ranchi credential — then ask for a booked follow-up.

drills.title: Discovery Depth: From 'What are you looking for?' to a Concrete Number
drills.instruction: Practice a 5-question discovery ladder: current role/tools → specific technical gap → target role and package (current vs expected LPA) → decision timeline → who else is part of the decision. Drill until you can reach a '4.5 to 8-10 LPA' style concrete framing within 4 minutes.

drills.title: Soft Close Under a 3-Minute Remaining Window
drills.instruction: Simulate a student who says 'I have to jump in 3 minutes.' Practice three micro-closes: (a) anchor a specific callback time, (b) offer the seat-block ₹4,000 path with a 10-day balance window, (c) summarise value in one sentence tied to their goal before asking. End each drill with a self-score on whether a follow-up time was actually locked.
